Bearing 315

The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest
July/August 1999

[Image: Stylized Native American formline art of a bear or wolf running with an orienteering map and a control flag in its mouth.]

Cascade and Sammamish Orienteering Clubs
would like to extend a special invitation to the three-day

1999 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival

this July 23-25, at Cle Elum, WA
(See enclosed flyer for more information!)

Upcoming Events

5 days of Class A meets in WA

Harvey Friedman

For the 1st time ever, orienteers in the state of Washington will have 5 days of nationally sanctioned meets without having to leave the state. In the Cle Elum environs, we will have meet days on the 23rd thru the 25th of July (yes, soon); in the eastern WA vicinity, 18th & 19th of September are the days for the meet near Sprague about 30 miles west of Spokane.

Why should I go, you might ask? Well, there are several good reasons that I can think of. One is that these will be top quality meets on interesting terrain. The courses will have been carefully prepared and checked (vetted) not just on the week before the meet as most local events usually turn out. You can register for the course for your age group that corresponds to any other at a nationally sanctioned meet. For those who are interested, going to 4 out of the 5 days and being a member of the United States Orienteering Federation (USOF) will enable anyone to be listed in the national rankings and published in the national orienteering journal (which one receives as a benefit of membership in USOF), if desired.

Another is that you can enjoy the fun & excitement of a relatively big meet (big for us but not like the 20000 who may the Swedish 5-day this year). It might be so much fun that you may want to travel to other big meets in Canada or California this September, or even fly to the East Coast, Europe, or elsewhere for orienteering. Meeting people from other areas is another good reason for attending. There are always people on a tight budget who camp and are usually quite friendly, and others who try to stay in motels with pool and/or jacuzzi, and even those (like me) who will probably commute for the Cle Elum meet.

For those who only go to meets in local parks, these 5 days will be a fun way for you to test how well you have developed orienteering skills. If you don’t care about ranking, O’ing by yourself, etc., you can still sign up for a recreational course and go as a whole family or just a couple of friends.

Orienteering - something for everyone!

Bearing 315 Editor Needed!

After 3 years of service current Bearing editor will be stepping down after the September/October 1999 issue. If you are interested in becoming the editor, contact either Donald Denbo (206) 366-0624 or Dave Tallent (206) 488-3691. The present editor is willing to help the new editor with the November/December 1999 issue.

In This Issue:

Submissions for the next issue must be received by Aug. 25, 1999. Send submissions to Don Denbo at dwd@halcyon.com or mail to 2138 N 186th St., Shoreline, WA, 98133.

Rooks Park

May 1, 1999
Walla Walla
Sacajawea Orienteers

Course Setters and Meet Directors: Sonny Elsasser and Frank Skorina
Control pickup: Sonny, Frank, and Michel Billaux
Weather: Raining while putting the flags out, settling to mostly cloudy and 60 degrees during the meet (i.e., perfect). Participants: 23

Experience won over youth--but not by much.

Conor Lucas-Roberts, although only a sophomore, is a standout on the WaHi cross-country team. Bob Gruel is a father, a husband, has a day job, and is a forty-something. Bob has been orienteering for years. Conor had been orienteering once.

Today it was Bob, finishing 30 seconds ahead of Conor. Participants were allowed to find as many controls that they had the desire and energy for, in any order. Bob, Conor, Conor’s twin brother Peter, Michel Billaux from Pasco, and brothers Mark and Ben VanDonge had the energy and found all 25. Teycee (rhymes with Stacy) Werner and Gabe Pimentel had the desire finding 20, solving problems at a walk on their first orienteering meet.

WaHi teacher Mrs. See (kinda like Mississippi but shorter) is credited with introducing eight of today’s participants to the sport. The opportunity of getting extra credit in her PE class helps our attendance. Thank you, Mrs. See!

Philip and Jane Schmitten celebrated 10 days of marital bliss with a map in hand. Unfortunately they choose a route with tightly packed contours back to the finish. Was Philip trying already to collect the insurance money? Let’s hope not. They are active members of the club and will help us go far.

Glen Cafferty of Portland came to Walla Walla to go orienteering. The day before he was setting courses for the Washington State Orienteering Championships in Goldendale to be held on the June 5-6 weekend. This meet will have easy and hard courses and is open to just about anyone. There will be extra amenities such as a shuttle to most starts, a great pre-printed map, and hobnobbing with those poor souls on the west side who have to endure all that rain! It will be fun, try it.

Horn Rapids County Park

May 15, 1999
Richland, WA
Sacajawea Orienteers

Course Setter and Meet Director: Michel Billaux
Control pickup: Michel, Bob, and Frank
Weather: Partly cloudly, temperature high 60’s to low 70’s; Participants: 17

Weather was great. Warm enough so you did not get cold standing around, and cool enough so your cells were not demanding hydration in order to continue navigation.

Bruce McAlister (the orienteering travel agent), Eric Bone (US Orienteering 'A' team member), and Taavi Tatsi (Estonian night orienteering champion) traveled over the mountains from the west side to visit our sagebrush. The Weakly-Cummings-Rutz bevy didn’t let a control go by with their 16 eyes scanning the bushes. And Christine will be taking her native French speaking skills to Puerto Rico this summer. We welcomed all the support her and her family have given to orienteering this past year.

I arrived at Horn Rapids to just ride the bike course and Bob came to just run the advanced course. He saw my bike and thought it would be interesting to give the wheels a try so we stage a little 'combined time' contest. After we got back from our planned courses, he borrowed my bike and I tightened my running shoes. On the second courses, he ran out of gas and I made a mental mistake on #7, but this time, I was to prevail.

Ros*Elum Ridge

May 16, 1999
Cle Elum, WA
Sammamish Orienteering Club

It was a rainy Sunday morning in Seattle, raining across the Pass, down past Easton, but just before Cle Elum, the rain stopped. At Ros*Elum, about 40 orienteers found cloudy to partly sunny skies, a light cool breeze, almost perfect running conditions.

Jan Urban had set four courses, all pre-printed on 1:10000 maps. There were lots of individual successes, but special mention goes to Eric Bone who ran course 4 in just over 57 minutes.

Helping were Dave Enger, Meet Director, Bob Reddick, Registration, Bruce McAlister Start/Finish and Eric Bone, Taavi Tatsi, Bill Cusworth and Roman and Martin Kotecky Marker Pickup.

Cornwall Park

May 23, 1999
Bellingham WA
Chuckanut Orienteering (CHUCKO)

Meet director, courses: Bill Green
Timing: Ken Klepsch
Registration, refreshments: Joanne Klepsch

Bill set the courses as a variation on a score-O. With 25 controls on the map, you could get 8 to finish on course 1, 16 on course 2 or 24 on course 3. You could pick which controls you got and which order you got them, like a score-O, but it was a timed event, like a regular O meet.

Boy Scout Troop 100 from Mt. Vernon took part in their first O’ meet. We also had Girl Scouts present. We extend a wetter and greener Western Washington welcome to Terradan Landchild and Jill Miller.

Howard Amon

May 25, 1999
Richland, WA
Sacajawea Orienteers

On a beautiful, sunny (yet windy) day eight (8) competitors met on our smallest venue, Howard Amon Park, within the city limits of Richland, WA. Ten seconds separated first and second place on the long course, with youth besting age and cunning, in this instance. The event teased two first-timers out to play. That was what this event was all about.

Pioneer Park

June 17, 1999
Walla Walla, WA
Sacajawea Orienteers

Course Setter and Meet Director: Steve Rapp
Control pickup: Steve Rapp, Pete Crawford, Daniel Gervais, Peter Lucas-Roberts, Frank Skorina
Weather: Great
Participants: 28

A great way to complete the spring season--a good turnout with lots of enthusiastic newcomers and a three-course combined competition between myself and WaHi student Peter Lucas-Roberts. This time my experience could not beat his enthusiasm.

We’ll hibernate local orienteering during the hot summer but will resume in September. The fall schedule will be decided shortly so get your requests in now.

I want to thank the participants, volunteers, and new members in making this spring season the best ever for Sacajawea Orienteers’!!

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don’t own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local (“C”) events with color maps. “A” and “B” meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.

Participant Type Fee
Club members (any club) $4.00
Non-club members $5.00
Junior (under 18) $3.00
Families map fee only after first two

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to “yousoff,” and you may have seen the magazine “Orienteering/North America.” But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn’t seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

United States Orienteering Federation
P.O. Box 1444
Forest Park, GA 30298
MEMBERSHIP APPLICATION

block_index.text: The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest July/August 1999
[Image: Stylized Native American formline art of a bear or wolf running with an orienteering map and a control flag in its mouth.]
Cascade and Sammamish Orienteering Clubs would like to extend a special invitation to the three-day
this July 23-25, at Cle Elum, WA (See enclosed flyer for more information!)
5 days of Class A meets in WA

For the 1st time ever, orienteers in the state of Washington will have 5 days of nationally sanctioned meets without having to leave the state. In the Cle Elum environs, we will have meet days on the 23rd thru the 25th of July (yes, soon); in the eastern WA vicinity, 18th & 19th of September are the days for the meet near Sprague about 30 miles west of Spokane.

Why should I go, you might ask? Well, there are several good reasons that I can think of. One is that these will be top quality meets on interesting terrain. The courses will have been carefully prepared and checked (vetted) not just on the week before the meet as most local events usually turn out. You can register for the course for your age group that corresponds to any other at a nationally sanctioned meet. For those who are interested, going to 4 out of the 5 days and being a member of the United States Orienteering Federation (USOF) will enable anyone to be listed in the national rankings and published in the national orienteering journal (which one receives as a benefit of membership in USOF), if desired.

Another is that you can enjoy the fun & excitement of a relatively big meet (big for us but not like the 20000 who may the Swedish 5-day this year). It might be so much fun that you may want to travel to other big meets in Canada or California this September, or even fly to the East Coast, Europe, or elsewhere for orienteering. Meeting people from other areas is another good reason for attending. There are always people on a tight budget who camp and are usually quite friendly, and others who try to stay in motels with pool and/or jacuzzi, and even those (like me) who will probably commute for the Cle Elum meet.

For those who only go to meets in local parks, these 5 days will be a fun way for you to test how well you have developed orienteering skills. If you don’t care about ranking, O’ing by yourself, etc., you can still sign up for a recreational course and go as a whole family or just a couple of friends.

After 3 years of service current Bearing editor will be stepping down after the September/October 1999 issue. If you are interested in becoming the editor, contact either Donald Denbo (206) 366-0624 or Dave Tallent (206) 488-3691. The present editor is willing to help the new editor with the November/December 1999 issue.

Copyright 1999, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club. Submissions for the next issue must be received by Aug. 25, 1999. Send submissions to Don Denbo at dwd@halcyon.com or mail to 2138 N 186th St., Shoreline, WA, 98133.
April 18, 1999 Redmond, WA. Sammamish Orienteering Club
Meet Director - Dave Enger Course Setter - John Sincock

Course Setters and Meet Directors: Sonny Elsasser and Frank Skorina Control pickup: Sonny, Frank, and Michel Billaux Weather: Raining while putting the flags out, settling to mostly cloudy and 60 degrees during the meet (i.e., perfect). Participants: 23
Experience won over youth--but not by much.

Conor Lucas-Roberts, although only a sophomore, is a standout on the WaHi cross-country team. Bob Gruel is a father, a husband, has a day job, and is a forty-something. Bob has been orienteering for years. Conor had been orienteering once.

Today it was Bob, finishing 30 seconds ahead of Conor. Participants were allowed to find as many controls that they had the desire and energy for, in any order. Bob, Conor, Conor’s twin brother Peter, Michel Billaux from Pasco, and brothers Mark and Ben VanDonge had the energy and found all 25. Teycee (rhymes with Stacy) Werner and Gabe Pimentel had the desire finding 20, solving problems at a walk on their first orienteering meet.

WaHi teacher Mrs. See (kinda like Mississippi but shorter) is credited with introducing eight of today’s participants to the sport. The opportunity of getting extra credit in her PE class helps our attendance. Thank you, Mrs. See!

Philip and Jane Schmitten celebrated 10 days of marital bliss with a map in hand. Unfortunately they choose a route with tightly packed contours back to the finish. Was Philip trying already to collect the insurance money? Let’s hope not. They are active members of the club and will help us go far.

Glen Cafferty of Portland came to Walla Walla to go orienteering. The day before he was setting courses for the Washington State Orienteering Championships in Goldendale to be held on the June 5-6 weekend. This meet will have easy and hard courses and is open to just about anyone. There will be extra amenities such as a shuttle to most starts, a great pre-printed map, and hobnobbing with those poor souls on the west side who have to endure all that rain! It will be fun, try it.
*Score* was calculated by awarding 1000 points for every control found and subtracting five points for every second over 75 minutes.
May 7, 1999 Shoreline, WA Cascade Orienteering Club

Over 80 runners (including 4 scout troops) showed up on a bright, cold day to run on Jim Siscel’s challenging and fun courses (including an OCAD mind trick, eliminating trails on the map for course 3).

Meet Directors: Katie and Doug Sprugel Course Design and Setting: Jim Siscel Map Production: Bill Cusworth, Eric Bone Registration: Robert Miller, Rick Breseman Beginner Instruction: Eric Bone, Katie Sprugel Starts: Don Atkinson, Ken Lew Finish/Results: Doug Sprugel, Arne Skog, Katie Sprugel Marker Pick-up: Doug Sprugel, Eric Bone, Bill Cusworth, Jim Siscel, Ben Sprugel
Andrew Merrill, Jared Grose & Chris Jones
Course 3 (4.45 k, 16 controls, no trails on map)

* One control on courses 3/4 and one on courses 2/3/4 were each not found by two of the more experienced orienteers. The map was less than complete in the neighborhood of one, and the other was a little hidden (due mainly to a lack of anything to tie the control to), so we decided not to disqualify these competitors.
Course Setter and Meet Director: Michel Billaux Control pickup: Michel, Bob, and Frank Weather: Partly cloudly, temperature high 60’s to low 70’s; Participants: 17
Weather was great. Warm enough so you did not get cold standing around, and cool enough so your cells were not demanding hydration in order to continue navigation.

Bruce McAlister (the orienteering travel agent), Eric Bone (US Orienteering 'A' team member), and Taavi Tatsi (Estonian night orienteering champion) traveled over the mountains from the west side to visit our sagebrush. The Weakly-Cummings-Rutz bevy didn’t let a control go by with their 16 eyes scanning the bushes. And Christine will be taking her native French speaking skills to Puerto Rico this summer. We welcomed all the support her and her family have given to orienteering this past year.

I arrived at Horn Rapids to just ride the bike course and Bob came to just run the advanced course. He saw my bike and thought it would be interesting to give the wheels a try so we stage a little 'combined time' contest. After we got back from our planned courses, he borrowed my bike and I tightened my running shoes. On the second courses, he ran out of gas and I made a mental mistake on #7, but this time, I was to prevail.
Combined Times for Bike & Run (21.1km, 27 cntrls)
May 16, 1999 Cle Elum, WA Sammamish Orienteering Club

It was a rainy Sunday morning in Seattle, raining across the Pass, down past Easton, but just before Cle Elum, the rain stopped. At Ros*Elum, about 40 orienteers found cloudy to partly sunny skies, a light cool breeze, almost perfect running conditions.
Jan Urban had set four courses, all pre-printed on 1:10000 maps. There were lots of individual successes, but special mention goes to Eric Bone who ran course 4 in just over 57 minutes.
Helping were Dave Enger, Meet Director, Bob Reddick, Registration, Bruce McAlister Start/Finish and Eric Bone, Taavi Tatsi, Bill Cusworth and Roman and Martin Kotecky Marker Pickup.
Meet director, courses: Bill Green Timing: Ken Klepsch Registration, refreshments: Joanne Klepsch

Bill set the courses as a variation on a score-O. With 25 controls on the map, you could get 8 to finish on course 1, 16 on course 2 or 24 on course 3. You could pick which controls you got and which order you got them, like a score-O, but it was a timed event, like a regular O meet.
Boy Scout Troop 100 from Mt. Vernon took part in their first O’ meet. We also had Girl Scouts present. We extend a wetter and greener Western Washington welcome to Terradan Landchild and Jill Miller.

On a beautiful, sunny (yet windy) day eight (8) competitors met on our smallest venue, Howard Amon Park, within the city limits of Richland, WA. Ten seconds separated first and second place on the long course, with youth besting age and cunning, in this instance. The event teased two first-timers out to play. That was what this event was all about.
Meet Director and Everything Else - Ivar Husa
Philip Warnick (first timer) with Seth and Brendan Higham
June 2, 1999 Bellevue, WA Sammamish Orienteering Club

Meet Director - Dave Enger Course Designer - John Sincock Course Setting & Pickup - Dave Enger Registration - Bob Reddick, Mark Howlett Timing - Dave Enger, Mark Howlett Bar-B-Que - Valerie Enger Weather - Sunny & Cool
June 17, 1999 Walla Walla, WA Sacajawea Orienteers
Course Setter and Meet Director: Steve Rapp Control pickup: Steve Rapp, Pete Crawford, Daniel Gervais, Peter Lucas-Roberts, Frank Skorina Weather: Great Participants: 28

A great way to complete the spring season--a good turnout with lots of enthusiastic newcomers and a three-course combined competition between myself and WaHi student Peter Lucas-Roberts. This time my experience could not beat his enthusiasm.
We’ll hibernate local orienteering during the hot summer but will resume in September. The fall schedule will be decided shortly so get your requests in now.
I want to thank the participants, volunteers, and new members in making this spring season the best ever for Sacajawea Orienteers’!!
Jeremy Davidson-McCloy, Mark McCloy, Catie Davidson, Peter and Meg Cochran, Ethan and Mona Bradley
Brienna Ayers, Heather Reeves, Roger Graves
Rick, Hitomi, & Cedric Johnson, Wendi Wolters
Dafoe, Bill Jr., Ilona, & Bill Sr. Bulen
Rick, Hitomi, & Cedric Johnson, Wendi Wolters
Jeremy Davidson-McCloy, Mark McCloy, Catie Davidson, Peter and Meg Cochran, Ethan and Mona Bradley
Brienna Ayers, Heather Reeves, Roger Graves
June 19, 1999 Seattle, WA Cascade Orienteering Club

Meet Director: Dave Tallent Courses: Eric Bone Marker placement: Eric Bone, Bill Cusworth Registration: Harvey Friedman Start: Ardis Dull, Debbie Newell, Jenny Pearson Finish: Don Atkinson, Dave Tallent Marker pickup: Don Denbo, Debbie Newell, Harvey Friedman
Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don’t own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local (“C”) events with color maps. “A” and “B” meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.
map fee only after first two
[ ] Cascade O.C. [ ] Sammamish O.C. [ ] Ellensburg O.C. [ ] Sacajawea O.C.

Orienteering Interests (circle): Event Participation | Improving Skills | Writing | Teaching Event Organization | Map Making | Publicity | Coaching Course Setting | Field Checking | Exploration of new areas

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to “yousoff,” and you may have seen the magazine “Orienteering/North America.” But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn’t seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
